coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are increasingly favoured in electric vehicles due to their large capacity, high power, and extended driving range. However, significant heat generation during operation poses challenges for efficient battery thermal management systems (BTMS). This study presents a numerical investigation of a BTMS using a mini-channel cooling block with water and Al2O3-GO-based mono and hybrid nanofluids. The impact of nanoparticle concentration, fluid velocity, and current discharge rate on the thermal performance of cylindrical 18650 LIBs were analyzed using computational fluid dynamics (CFD). Thermal conductivity and dynamic viscosity of the Al2O3-GO nanofluids were experimentally evaluated. A novel cooling block featuring 5 mm diameter circular channels was designed and validated against experimental data, showing a maximum deviation of 0.26 °C. The results demonstrated significant temperature uniformity across LIB cells, with the hybrid nanofluid at 1 % volume concentration enhancing thermal conductivity by 42.5 % and reducing LIB temperature by 7.47 %. Increasing fluid velocity from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s further improved cooling by 7.53 %. The study concluded that Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluids are highly effective for BTMS, even under high current discharge rates, due to their enhanced heat transfer properties.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are increasingly favoured in electric vehicles due to their large capacity, high power, and extended driving range. However, significant heat generation during operation poses challenges for efficient battery thermal management systems (BTMS). This study presents a numerical investigation of a BTMS using a mini-channel cooling block with water and Al2O3-GO-based mono and hybrid nanofluids. The impact of nanoparticle concentration, fluid velocity, and current discharge rate on the thermal performance of cylindrical 18650 LIBs were analyzed using computational fluid dynamics (CFD). Thermal conductivity and dynamic viscosity of the Al2O3-GO nanofluids were experimentally evaluated. A novel cooling block featuring 5 mm diameter circular channels was designed and validated against experimental data, showing a maximum deviation of 0.26 °C. The results demonstrated significant temperature uniformity across LIB cells, with the hybrid nanofluid at 1 % volume concentration enhancing thermal conductivity by 42.5 % and reducing LIB temperature by 7.47 %. Increasing fluid velocity from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s further improved cooling by 7.53 %. The study concluded that Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluids are highly effective for BTMS, even under high current discharge rates, due to their enhanced heat transfer properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The transportation sector contributes about 24 % of overall carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions, and 75 % of them are from road transport. Crude oil depletion and CO2 emission are rising with the increase in automobile sales [1,2]. This is caused by the fossil fuel combustion by conventional internal combustion engines (ICE) that propel the automotive industry. The rise of transportation growth and high fuel consumption triggered the automobile industry to replace ICE engines with motors using batteries as power sources, called green vehicles [3]. Green vehicles such as electric vehicles (EVs), plug-in electric vehicles (PHEVs), and fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) use batteries with large capacity, high power, and extended driving range. Even though the use of green vehicles in the market is still slow due to their high manufacturing cost, low production, low availability of charging stations, low consumers, and autonomy [4,5], most countries have made considerable investments to replace conventional ICE vehicles to green vehicles as part of decarbonization effort. In the last two decades, many rechargeable batteries like Ni-based, Ni-MH, lead acid, and lithium ion based were employed for vehicle drivetrains empowerment. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are recognized as a reliable energy source for HEVs and EVs, among others, due to their low discharge rate, high energy density, and long lifespan [6]. However, the operation method leads to high heat generation by the battery when charged/discharged at a higher rate [5,7]. The thermal behavior depends on battery cell efficiency, working voltage, and available capacity. Suppose the battery pack design is inappropriate or has a poor thermal management system. In that case, it will cause the overall temperature to rise, and working at high temperatures will affect the battery performance and lead to degradation. Furthermore, it will speed up the battery aging and lead to thermal runaway [8].

The optimum working temperature of LIB is between 20 °C and 40 °C, and it must not exceed 50 °C in the long run. The maximum temperature difference must be no more than 5 °C to prevent the aging process from accelerating, which eventually causes the actual loss of useable battery capacity [8]. Therefore, BTMS is critical to control heat dissipation, ensuring the battery pack performs at an ideal temperature range and constantly dissipates the heat generated [3]. Appropriate thermal management systems are crucial to improve battery performance and lifespan. Several BTMS were studied using various materials and cooling mediums. For example, the cooling system uses a liquid cold plate (LCP), phase change material (PCM), heat pipe, and heat sink. Meanwhile, air, water, conventional fluid, and advanced nanofluid were used as cooling mediums.

For instance, Huo et al. [9] investigated LCP multiple channel structures for LIB cooling systems and found an improvement in flow rate and thermal management of the battery if the number of channels. Jin et al. [10] used LCP with an ultra-thin mini channel and found thermal performance enhanced because of the channel's thinner boundary layer and its flow. Jarret and Kin et al. [11] invented serpentine subchannel LCP and found they could prevent the LIB temperature from rising, but thermal non-uniformity occurred. Yet, the LCP is unsatisfactory due to its shape concern, and a more flexible structure is needed, especially for cylindrical batteries. Therefore, an LCP cooling system is not recommended for cylindrical LIBs [12].

PCMs are used for phase change cooling in battery temperature control, and they can absorb large amounts of heat energy while maintaining a constant temperature and work passively [13]. Li et al. [14] used PCM as a heat transfer medium in a numerical simulation study in which a battery pack was attached to PCM, and air and silicon oil flowed through the cooling channels. They state that silicon oil has moderate cooling performance, but due to its low thermal conductivity, it is less effective in conducting the heat away from the PCM material. Hence, the PCM application has some limitations for applications such as BTMS. In another study by Joshy et al. [15] on battery cooling using PCM, the researchers found that the amplitude and frequency of vibration could affect cooling performance and battery temperature. They also found that low heat capacity and thermal conductivity of air could not control battery thermal runaway. Bibin et al. [16] conducted a study by utilizing heat pipes for LIB cooling performance, and their result showed that heat pipes can perform well for long-term operation when the surrounding area has low temperatures. Besides that, heat pipes have excellent thermal conductivity. They are broadly utilized in many industries, but heat pipes are not favorable for EV applications due to their high cost and their process of gravity, which moves at various amplitudes [13]. A multi-electrochemical thermal model was introduced by Liang et al. [17], who utilized heat pipes with liquid cooling. The study result revealed that the maximum temperature of the module was decreased but found a reduction in coolant temperature and a rise in module temperature difference.

Zhao et al. [18] performed a numerical simulation on wavy channel design to investigate the thermal performance of a cylindrical LIB module with 71 18650 LIB cells. The study results show that at a higher current discharge rate, the neighboring battery heat exchange area rises to a high temperature and violates the LIB module's thermal equilibrium. Wang et al. [19] used a parallel cooling system and conducted a numerical investigation and experimental investigation on thermal efficiency with the effect of cooling mode. The author stated that it performed better than series cooling by promoting the temperature difference of the battery module. Micro-channel liquid cooling was introduced by Xie et al. [20], and they found improvement in convective heat transfer within the cooling channel. It could affect the temperature uniformity of the battery cells in the pack.

Hybrid BTMS was developed by Kiani et al. [21] using alumina nanofluid cooling and PCM with copper foam saturated, and the author found that water cooling could not maintain the temperature rise. Still, nanofluid could keep the battery temperature within safe conditions. Battery operating time was prolonged by 29 % when nanofluid was employed as coolant, whereas the battery operating time was sustained by 4 % only for water. A comparison of battery thermal performance at constant current discharge rates of 1C, 2C, and 3C, investigated by Liu et al. [22] using different base fluids, such as water, ethylene glycol, and engine oil, comparable to nanofluid as coolant. The result revealed thermal performance improved after adding Al2O3 nanoparticles into the base fluids at 0.1 m/s of fluid velocity. Kiani et al. [23] employed CuO, Al2O3, and AgO at nanoparticle volume concentrations between 1 and 4% for their battery thermal management system. They reported that using AgO/water nanofluid showed a significant reduction in battery temperature and maintained the safe condition for a longer duration. They concluded that CuO, Al2O3, and AgO nanoparticles can improve the thermal performance of batteries by 23.8 %, 40.5 %, and 45.2 %, respectively [24].

Titanium oxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO) and diamond-based nanofluids with water/ethylene (EG) base fluid were investigated by Li et al. [20] and found that all fluid has comparable viscosity but nanofluid with metal-based additives performed better than others due to its high thermal conductivity. Asokan et al. [25] utilized Al2O3-CuO mono and hybrid nanofluid with low concentration to experiment with the heat exchange performance, and the result showed 0.06 % concentration of hybrid nanofluid has better thermal conductivity than mono nanofluid which is about 1.3 %, and they maintained the 50:50 ratio of the nanoparticle. They also reported that the heat transfer coefficient was enhanced from 7.2 % to 12.1 %. The aluminum oxide/silicon oxide Al2O3-SiO2 hybrid nanofluids with green bio glycol was investigated by Izwan et al. [26], found thermal conductivity was increased by 21.2 % compared to the base fluid. Besides that, Al2O3-SiO2 with 30:70 ratio was most suitable even though they found a significant raise in nanofluid viscosity [26]. Therefore, the nanoparticle ratio is vital for hybrid nanofluids to achieve better cooling performance.

Many studies show that Al2O3-based nanofluids are the most promising as they are more stable nanofluids with good thermal properties when cross-breed with base fluids [27–29]. On the other side, graphene oxide particles also have a high potential for heat transfer applications like PV panel cooling, as stated by Yu et al. [30] but due to the poor stability during suspension in base fluid, graphene oxide (GO) is ignored most of the time. Sarode et al. [31] synthesized GO-CuO nanofluid using ultrasonic and concluded that thermal properties have improved. In a recent study, Kamalnayan et al. [32] investigated 0.01 %–0.1 % volume concentration of reduced graphene oxide (rGO)-Al2O3 and reported that when 0.05 % volume concentration (rGO)-Al2O3 flowing at turbulent flow, found high heat transfer coefficient at the outlet of the test section. Apart from types of cooling fluid, it is crucial to consider the concentration of cooling fluid and the velocity when it comes to BTMS. In the literature by Alqaed et al. [33], the authors stated that, convective heat transfer coefficient increases if the fluid velocity and nanoparticle concentration increase.

One thing to note from past research is that the authors focus on low-concentration hybrid nanofluids. Also, hybrid Al2O3-GO composition at higher concentrations was not explored in depth, especially for LIB cooling. The present work uses a cooling block with parallel mini-cooling channels approach to simulate the effect of volume concentrations of Al2O3-GO hybrid and 1.0 % of mono nanofluids with an 80:20 nanoparticle ratio, the effect of velocity (0.1 m/s and 0.3 m/s) and the effect of different battery discharge rate (0.5C, 1C and 2C) on the cooling performance of LIB cells. This study focuses on the thermal management system during the discharging process because LIB releases higher heat due to the exothermic reaction. The CFD numerical analysis is used to compute the heat transfer coefficient and Nusselt number. On the other side, an experimental work of this cooling system consisting of 20 LIBs connected in a 4S5P arrangement was conducted as a validation of numerical analysis with physical reality and the battery temperature rises for each condition were recorded. The heat transfer coefficient of working fluids was analyzed in both simulation and experiment work. Moreover, the Reynolds number and Nusselt number are calculated from both simulation and experimental results to identify the working fluid flow in the mini channels. At the end of this study, the cooling performance of LIB using mono and hybrid nanofluid was explored and the suitable volume concentration of nanofluid at different fluid velocities and battery discharging C- rates was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery pack design with cooling block
ce:section.ce:para: The liquid cooling system was designed using Solidworks version 2021 software for battery thermal management. This system has a rectangular cooling block with a fixed 180-degree contact surface for 20 battery cells. Panasonic NCR 18650-type cylindrical lithium-ion battery cells with c-shaped aluminum battery tabs which connect the top and bottom of the battery attached to both sides’ curves of the cooling block. Five hollow structures of 5 mm diameter were modelled as parallel mini-cooling channels. The three-dimensional design of the battery cooling module is displayed in Fig. 1 . The LIB cell modules are designed to be 18 mm in diameter and 65 mm in height. The cooling block length, width, and height are 256 mm, 34 mm, and 65 mm, respectively. The curvature of the battery module is designed with a 9 mm radius each to fit 20 battery cells. Aluminum is a cooling block material due to its low specific heat capacity. A total of 20 LIB cells are used, and each cell has a nominal capacity of 3.25 Ah and a nominal voltage of 3.7V. The length and diameter of each circular channel are 256 mm long and 5 mm diameter. A total of 5 channels were extruded in the block with a 2.5 mm gap between each channel. The actual battery cooling system will have many batteries, and the battery packs will face complexity in the cooling simulation. Therefore, a mini cooling system of 20 battery cells is employed in this work for low complexity, low cost, energy, and time savings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and thermophysical properties of hybrid and mono nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: The Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluids are prepared using a well-established two-step method at particle volume concentrations of 0.25 %–1.0 % and deionized water (DI) as base fluid. As for the mono nanofluids, the volume concentrations of Al2O3 and GO nanoparticles are set at 1.0 %. The mixture ratio of Al2O3 and GO is 80:20 for all hybrid nanofluid concentrations. The 80:20 ratio of Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluids is investigated because it revealed the highest zeta potential value with good visual stability after 30 days of observation period [34]. Hexadecyltrimethylammoniumbromide (CTAB) was employed as a surfactant to ensure homogenous mixing of nanoparticles in the base fluid to improve nanofluid stability. CTAB surfactant has the advantage of improving the stability of nanofluid. This has been proven in the literature of investigation on various surfactant to stabilize the nanofluids by Xin et al. [35] They reported that CTAB shown comparable absorbance in nanofluid and a better stability than other surfactants. The preparation process of mono and hybrid nanofluids involves a mechanical stirring process for 1 h 30 min, followed by probe ultrasonication for 1.5 h and bath ultrasonication for another 1.5 h. The prepared samples were stored in airtight containers and left for 2 weeks to observe the nanofluid stability. Fig. 2 shows the sedimentation observation of nanofluid samples after two weeks. The visual observation indicates that the GO mono solution stability failed after 14 days, and the rest of the nanofluid samples were well achieved.

The thermophysical properties, dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity were tested in this study. The dynamic viscosity was measured for both mono and hybrid nanofluids using an Anton Paar Rheometer for temperatures of 30 °C–70 °C. The thermal conductivity of nanofluids is vital among all thermophysical properties investigations because it plays a crucial role in a battery's thermal management system. The thermal conductivity of nanofluids is measured for temperatures of 30 °C–70 °C using a Tempos thermal analyzer.

Computational fluid dynamics solver, Ansys fluent workbench version 2023 software, is used for the simulation work. Cooling block geometry was saved in (STEP) file format in Solidworks and imported into Ansys fluent workbench. The total number of geometry bodies was identified by selecting each surface and the body of the geometry and then naming them with part names such as the battery, tabs, cooling block, channels, inlet, and outlet. Two thermocouple walls were developed to show Ansys fluent computation to neglect the wall between the batteries and cooling block, cooling block, and cooling channels to allow the heat from battery and tabs to flow freely to the channel without any barrier.

The simulation setup was initiated by using the required parameters and boundary conditions. Firstly, the material of the cooling block was defined as aluminum, and the physical properties of the cooling block were set automatically once the material was aluminum. The thermophysical properties are assumed to be constant throughout the simulation. The thermal physical properties and parameters of LIB are set as shown in Table 1 , which displays the properties of the Panasonic NCR 18650 B LIB cell used in the simulation model. The heat flux was computed using the specification data sheet with a specific heat capacity of 2400J/Kg.K [36,37]. The radiation heat transfer is assumed to be negligible during the discharging process, and the internal battery is constantly heated. Since different materials are in the cooling system, it was considered one material to simplify the computational model. A few assumptions were made based on the previous work by Zhenwei et al. [38] to streamline the governing equations (2) and (3) and the energy conservation equation (1) as displayed.
•
No electrode volume changes during battery discharging process.
•
During the discharge process, no air or gas is released.
•
All other reactions are ignored.
•
Thermal resistance by contacting various materials is neglected.
•
No thermal radiation of battery during discharging
•
The batteries are adiabatic and there is no heat loss to surrounding.

The energy source for the battery was selected as the main aim is to investigate the heat transfer from the battery to the cooling fluid at LIB constant current discharge rates of 0.5C, 1C, and 2C. The heat flux of a single LIB cell at a constant current discharge rate of 0.5C, 1C, and 2C was calculated using Equation (2) [39]. Then, energy flux was calculated by using Equation (3) [12], while the discharging time based on C-rates are shown in Table 2 .

Next, the material of the cooling block was set as aluminium, and the physical properties were updated by Ansys fluent by default. Then, water was set as the cooling fluid at first. The thermophysical properties of water, such as density, specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, and viscosity, were input under cell zone conditions. The same procedure was repeated when nanofluid was used as a cooling fluid. As the water is an incompressible fluid, the Governing equations were derived below in equations (4)–(6) [38].

For boundary conditions, two different working fluid velocities of 0.1 m/s and 0.3 m/s were investigated at ambient conditions. This two fluid velocity was considered mainly because the focus of present work is investigate the effect of hybrid nanofluid and the cooling effect at a higher and also a much lower fluid velocity. Since most literature suggest standard fluid inlet velocity for battery thermal management system ranging from 0.05 to 0.5 m/s, the basic for cooling fluid velocity is set at 0.1 m/s. Then, the velocity was increased by 3 times to 0.3 m/s to observe the battery cooling performance for different nanofluids. The battery temperature rise at minimum and maximum fluid velocity were studied. The initial temperature of LIB and working fluid was set at 298K during each simulation run. The fluid pressure at the inlet and outlet of the channels was neglected and set at zero. All outer surfaces of the battery module were assumed to be fully insulated, and there was no heat loss to the surroundings. So, the heat dissipated from battery and tabs transferred by one way which is from battery to cooling fluid since thermocouple wall have been created to prevent any heat concentration at wall of battery. The boundary condition for battery was assumed one which is the amount of heat flux.

Reynold's number (Re) for fluid was calculated to identify the type of flow, whether laminar or turbulence, using equation (7). At a maximum velocity of 0.3 m/s, the Re for base fluid, 1 % of GO, 1 % of Al2O3, 0.25 %,0.5 %,0.75 % and 1 % of Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid are 1924, 1992, 1902, 1731, 1675, 1537and 1435 respectively. It can be found that the highest Re is 1992.3 among all working fluids, which is less than 2300. Therefore, the working fluid flow is defined as laminar flow due to a lower Reynolds number computed (<2300) [13]. Meanwhile, the simulation computational parameter was set as 900 for the number of time steps, 1 for time step size, and 5 for maximum iteration. This indicates that the cooling simulation with water as a working fluid was run 5 times with a running time of 900s for each test run. Then, simulation work was repeated with Al2O3 and GO mono and hybrid nanofluid as the cooling fluid.

Next, meshing was made for the geometry by default, and the first meshing had 525,569 elements. The meshing refinement was done to reduce the notes into smaller sizes to let the Ansys software identify the small geometry bodies, curves, and edges to run the simulation. Also, meshing is done to get accurate results as it affects CFD simulation's accuracy, speed, and convergence. The grid-independent analysis was done by simulating LIB cooling without any fluid at a 0.5C discharge rate with different mesh sizes. Table 3 shows the number of meshing elements and the percentage difference in temperature data for different mesh sizes. The tabulated data shows a maximum and minimum temperature difference of 0.65 % and 0.06 % obtained for the smallest meshing elements of 525,569 and highest elements of 2,677,381, respectively. Therefore, the final meshing size was set as 2,182,256 elements with 451,364 notes due to its low temperature rise and maintained constant temperature rise after refining the meshing sizes. Fig. 3 revealed the cooling block and batteries with meshing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup of LIB cooling system for validation purpose
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental setup for the LIB cooling system consists of an aluminum cooling block, 4S5P battery module, Pico data logger, K-type thermocouples, electronic load, pump, and water reservoir, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The purpose of the setup is to validate the simulation model with experimental cooling data using water as the working fluid. The temperature of LIB batteries, cooling block, and water was measured using 12 K-type thermocouples. All thermocouples were connected to the data logger and computer to record the temperature rise using Picolog TCO8 software [36]. The cooling block with batteries was insulated with super wool to prevent heat loss to the surroundings. The reservoir container was filled with 1L of water, and the pump flow rate was set to 0.6 LPM, which indicates 0.1 m/s. The discharge meter is connected to battery terminals and has started discharging at a 0.5C rate with a 7.5A current. The water pumped from the reservoir flows through the hose pipe and cooling channels and back to the reservoir while the batteries are discharged. A cooling fan was placed near the reservoir container to maintain the water temperature around 25 ± 0.5 °C. These procedures were repeated for nanofluids as cooling fluid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat transfer coefficient and Nusselt number of working fluids
ce:section.ce:para:
During battery discharging simulation, the cooling fluid temperature changes from the inlet to the outlet of the channel are monitored and recorded for each working fluid at different C- rate and velocity. The heat transfer coefficient and Nusselt number of other working fluids were calculated by employing Equation (8) and Equation (12) [40,41]. The bulk temperature is the average of both inlet and outlet temperature, where the surface temperature of the channel is measured at three areas of the channels and averaged for heat transfer coefficient calculation.

Fig. 5 demonstrates the effect of temperature on the dynamic viscosity of Al2O3-GO mono and hybrid nanofluids. The dynamic viscosity measurement for all nanofluid samples is significantly higher at a temperature of 30 °C than the dynamic viscosity recorded at 70 °C. The lowest dynamic viscosity value was obtained for GO mono nanofluid, which is about 0.783 mPa s when the fluid temperature is 70 °C. The highest dynamic viscosity about 1.087 mPa s was observed for Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid, 1.0 % volume concentration at a temperature of 30 °C. The increase in nanofluid temperature decreased fluid viscosity as the temperature was raised gradually from 30 °C to 70 °C. Furthermore, the increase in hybrid nanofluid volume concentrations also indicates a rise in dynamic viscosity.

However, the increase in viscosity for hybrid nanofluid was suppressed by the temperature effect. The maximum reduction in viscosity is for mono GO nanofluid, which is about 18.1 %. Among all four volume concentrations, the Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid with 0.25 % particle concentrations exhibited the maximum reduction in viscosity with a reduction of 16.5 %. The decrease in nanofluid viscosity was due to the thermophysical properties of the base fluid and the homogenous suspension pattern of Al2O3-GO nanoparticles in the base fluid. The effect of temperature and base fluid properties on the viscosity reduction of nanofluids was clearly explained in a study conducted by Praveen et al. [42]. An increase in temperature affects the Al2O3-GO nanoparticles to absorb energy and improve particle movement because of the Van der Wall forces alteration.

This also helps to reduce particle agglomeration. Meanwhile, at high viscosity, the liquid has a strong intermolecular force that holds out the fluid flow [42]. The base fluid dynamic viscosity reduction is also one of the reasons for the nanofluid viscosity drop. Based on Fig. 5, it is observed that the dynamic viscosity for DI water drops by 44.9 % with the rise in temperature from 30 °C to 70 °C.

Fig. 6 shows that the thermal conductivity of mono nanofluid and hybrid nanofluid increases steadily as the temperature is raised from 30 °C to a maximum value of 70 °C. The thermal conductivity data suggest a strong influence of temperature in the thermal conductivity enhancement for Al2O3-GO mono and hybrid nanofluids. The Al2O3 hybrid nanofluid recorded a higher thermal conductivity than mono Al2O3 and GO nanofluids. At peak temperature 70 °C, the highest thermal conductivity value recorded is 1.192W/m.K for 1.0 % volume concentrations of Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid, and the lowest thermal conductivity is about 0.790W/m.K for GO mono nanofluid. Overall, the thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids increases with temperature due to the Brownian motion of these two different components of nanoparticles. The molecules move freely and create a convectional current, demonstrating higher thermal conductivity than mono nanofluid [23]. The highest thermal conductivity of Al2O3 mono nanofluid at 70 °C is 0.875W/m.K, about 12.5 % higher than GO mono nanofluid.

The Al2O3 and GO mono nanofluid recorded thermal conductivity improvement of 18.9 % and 18.3 %, respectively, from 30 to 70 °C. When observed for water the maximum thermal conductivity at 70 °C is about 0.664W/m.K. The increment of water thermal conductivity is just by 7.4 % only from 30 °C to 70 °C which indicates the least thermal conductivity enhancement when compared to mono and hybrid nanofluid. The trendline pattern for water, mono and hybrid nanofluids is linear with temperature. However, the slope of the lines for all four hybrid nanofluids slightly increases in the middle of the temperature rise. In contrast, the 1.0 % Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid thermal conductivity slowly increased by about 9.88 % until 50 °C and rapidly rose to about 23.14 % as the temperature reached 70 °C. It can be seen from Fig. 6 that the thermal conductivity enhancement for 1.0 % of Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid at 30 °C–70 °C temperature is 27.86 %, 16.42 % and 17.04 % higher than water, Al2O3 and GO mono nanofluid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uncertainty analysis of dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity
ce:section.ce:para: The absolute uncertainty in the experimental measurement of dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity are presented in Table 4 for fluid temperature of 70 °C. The calculated data revealed the measured data of dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity indicates an absolute uncertainty of ±0.001 or 0.1 %. A low uncertainty percentage shows that the measured value has high precision with minimal deviation from the true value. This indicates minor uncertainty leading to a minimal error percentage during the measurement process. The accuracy of the thermal conductivity data is significantly high as the instrument was first calibrated using glycerin and it was found to attain measurement uncertainty of less than 3 % and this follow the manufacturers standard which allows a 5 % maximum uncertainty. Besides, a minimum time interval of 20 min between each measurement ensures a stable and reliable data measurement with average values presented for the analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature of LIB module without cooling effect
ce:section.ce:para: The LIB cooling system was first simulated without working fluid at constant current discharge rates of 0.5 C, 1C and 2C to observe the thermal behaviour of LIB cells and determine the maximum rise in temperature for a period of 900s. Fig. 7 represents the rise in battery module temperature influenced by the discharge rate at every 180s time step. The trendline describes that the battery temperature almost rises linearly with time. The maximum temperature recorded for LIB cells at each current discharge rate is 29 °C, 32 °C and 36 °C, respectively. The highest temperature was recorded for a current discharge rate of 2C due to the higher current load of 6.6A, generating higher heat energy. The electrical energy that is used by the LIB cell is converted to heat energy and released from the battery during the discharge process. When compared to a previous study by Mitra et al. [24], they found that the battery temperature for the modules with serpentine channels without cooling fluid at discharge rates of 0.5C, 1.5C and 2.1C were about 37.9 °C, 40.°C and 42.7 °C, respectively, after 600s.

The temperature contour of the battery module and cooling block without liquid cooling effect are displayed in Fig. 8 . The temperature contour illustration shows the peak temperature range obtained with the increase in the current discharge rate from 0.5C to a maximum discharge rate of 2C. Heat energy is dissipated from the LIB to the aluminum block, which serves as a heat exchanger. The colour gradient describes the temperature variation of the battery module. The royal blue region indicates minimum temperature, which is room temperature, and the red region indicates a higher temperature region. Based on Fig. 8, the battery area became warmer after battery modules discharged at 0.5C,1C and 2C. It can be observed that the heat dissipates from the battery modules to the aluminum cooling block and remains in the cooling block, keeping the center area of the block at a higher temperature gradient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of numerical model with experimental data
ce:section.ce:para: The simulation of the battery discharging process was done with water as a cooling fluid for 900s. For the experimental validation, the battery modules were discharged at 0.5C (7.5A) for nine hundred seconds while water was pumped into the cooling channels at a flow rate of 0.6L/min. The average temperature rise from all thermocouple readings were compared with simulation data. Fig. 9 compares LIB temperature from simulation and experimental cooling using water as a working fluid at a discharge rate of 0.5C for 900s. Fig. 9 indicates a similar trend line for LIB temperature plotted from simulation and experimental data. The maximum temperature of THE battery when water was used as working fluid from simulation and experimental data was 25.42 °C and 25.63 °C, respectively.

For instance, the rise in LIB temperature during the cooling simulation is about 1.68 %, while the experimental cooling data indicates a 2.06 % rise from the initial temperature. The deviation between the experimental and simulation battery temperature rise is about 0.37 %. Whereas for the 1 % of mono and hybrid nanofluid, the temperature difference from simulation and experimental were about 0.16 %, 0.08 % and 0.14 %. The maximum battery temperature difference during discharge at 0.5C is about 0.83 % which is at 900 s when water is used. For mono and hybrid nanofluid the maximum temperature difference was 0.79 %, 0.46 % and 0.24 % at 900s. This validates the accuracy of the numerical model developed. Also, it was found that both simulation and experimental temperature results were similar at every stage of the discharge process. From Fig. 9 the LIB temperature recorded from practical work shows a slightly higher temperature gradient than the simulated temperature data.

Even for current discharge rates of 1C and 2C, the experimental battery temperature rise is like simulation data at the same fluid velocity (0.1 m/s). For the 1C rate, battery temperature has the highest difference about 0.95 %,0.8 % for water, 1.0 % of GO and 0.89 % for Al2O3 mono nanofluid, 1.0 % of Al2O3-GO as shown in Fig. 10 . Whereas for the 2C rate, it can be found 0.010 °C difference for 1.0 % of both Al2O3, GO mono nanofluid and 0.008 °C for 1.0 % of Al2O3-GO as the maximum that is shown in Fig. 11 . Overall, at these three-discharge rates (0.5C,1C,2C) the percentage difference between simulation and experimental battery temperature rise is within 1 % which indicates smaller margin of deviation between simulation and experimental data. The maximum error value is about 0.99 %. The slight error difference in LIB temperature from experimental data is due to the battery configurations of 4S5P and the variation of the thermocouple placement. Besides, the cooling fluid temperature fluctuation before flowing into the battery cooling block caused a temperature variation as the working fluid was recirculated in a closed-loop system. The experimental recirculation system was tested multiple times and the inlet temperature of liquid stored in the reservoir tank was measured using multiple thermocouple to ensure minimal error in temperature data. Furthermore, a cooling fan was used to unsure the absolute uncertainly for inlet temperature is ±0.5 °C. Therefore, it is crucial to maintain the water temperature at the reservoir container at 25 °C. It can be observed that the difference between simulation and experimental temperature rise for higher discharge rates are comparable with low discharge rates. However, the battery temperature rises with water, mono and hybrid nanofluid in simulation and experiment are similar within acceptable range of difference. This proves that the validation of simulation and experimental result is passed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation of battery discharging process with cooling fluids
Effect of Al2O3 and GO concentrations on LIB cooling
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Al2O3-GO nanoparticle concentrations on the cooling performance of LIB cells was numerically investigated for both mono and hybrid nanofluid at a constant current discharge rate of 0.5C, 1C and 2C and constant fluid velocity of 0.1 m/s. The heat generated by the battery module is dispersed to the aluminum cooling block and removed by the cooling fluid flow. Fig. 12 shows the rise in temperature of the battery module for cooling at 0.1 m/s using mono and hybrid nanofluids at current discharge rates of 0.5C. The highest temperature of LIB was recorded when water was used as the working fluid for all discharge rates. Both mono and hybrid nanofluids show a significant reduction in battery temperature compared to the results obtained for DI water. For instance, mono Al2O3 and GO nanofluid reduced the battery temperature by about 3.72 °C and 3.73 °C. This corresponds to an average cooling improvement of 12.8 % using mono nanofluids compared to base fluid. Furthermore, the use of hybrid Al2O3-GO nanofluid shows improved battery temperature reduction with increased Al2O3-GO concentrations. The lowest battery temperature of 25.22 °C was recorded employing a hybrid nanofluid with 1 % particle concentrations with a cooling enhancement of 13.03 % compared to the performance of base fluid water. The effect of Al2O3-GO concentrations on the cooling performance of LIB is increased significantly from 0s towards the end of the cooling cycle at 900s. Based on Fig. 12 cooling curve, it is observed that for 0.25 % volume concentration of Al2O3-GO the battery temperature rise was about 1.0 %. Whereas for 1.0 % volume concentration of Al2O3-GO the battery temperature rise was about 0.8 %. It can be found that the battery temperature difference between the lowest and highest volume concentration of hybrid nanofluid is about 0.2 %. Even for 1C and 2C discharge rates, the battery temperature difference between 0.25 % and 1.0 % volume concentration of Al2O3-GO was found to be 0.2 % and 0.9 %. It can be verified that the battery cooling performance is improving along with increasing the hybrid nanofluid volume concentrations.

However, for higher current discharge rates of 1C and 2C, the maximum temperature recorded by the LIB cell is about 32 °C and 36 °C, respectively. The cooling simulation using a hybrid nanofluid with 1 % Al2O3-GO nanoparticle concentration reduced LIB's temperature to a minimum value of 25.8 °C and 28.6 °C. It corresponds to a 19.5 % and 20.7 % cooling enhancement for 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively. Hybrid nanofluids resulted in a significant enhancement in cooling performance for higher discharge rates, as illustrated in Figs. 13 and 14
. The LIB cooling simulation results obtained for the Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid are much better than those of the mono nanofluid and base fluid, mainly due to the higher thermal conductivity of the hybrid nanofluid causing the Brownian motion effect. The enhancement in thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid due to higher concentrations of Al2O3-GO nanoparticles significantly contributed to better cooling performance. For example, a 42.5 % enhancement in thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid (1 %) relative to the base fluid can reduce LIB's temperature by almost 7.47 % at a 2C discharge rate and flow velocity of 0.1 m/s. The effect of Brownian motion in thermal conductivity alteration improved the energy and momentum exchange between nanoparticles in water, aiding the heat transfer from LIB cells. A similar finding was reported by Kiani et al. [23] in which the authors observed an almost 77 % reduction in battery temperature when the concentration of AgO nanofluid increased to about 2 %. Furthermore, the convection flow of fluid will be influenced by the rise in temperature. This will lead to thermophoresis occurrence when there is particle collision from high to low temperature and the nanoparticle moves directly [43]. The present findings are substantial to the results obtained by.

In another numerical investigation on LIB using SiO2-water nanofluid by Husam et al. [44], the authors reported a 4K temperature reduction with a maximum particle concentration of 5 %. However, the use of high concentration nanofluid has a negative effect due to the increase in nanofluid viscosity with particle concentrations, which may lead to higher pumping power in the cooling system. Therefore, the present investigation work uses a 1 % hybrid combination of Al2O3-GO (80:20) with slower fluid flow rate which does not require high pumping power due to smaller viscosity. The battery cooling efficiency of the highest concentration of hybrid nanofluid is better even at a low flow rate. The present work showed better results than recent literature that uses mono nanofluid with high particle concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Reynolds number on LIB cooling at different fluid velocities
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of fluid velocity on the LIB cooling was investigated for Al2O3-GO nanofluids and its base fluid at Re ranging between 450 and 2000. The cooling simulation was performed with battery discharge rates of 0.5C, 1C and 2C. Fig. 15 illustrates the average battery temperature when cooled with different working fluids at 0.3 m/s and 0.5C. The results show a substantial improvement in the cooling performance at a higher fluid flow of 0.3 m/s. The increase in fluid velocity from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s is able to suppress the battery temperature even at a high discharge rate of 2C when using a hybrid nanofluid as the cooling fluid. For a lower current discharge rate of 0.5C, when water is pumped to the cooling block at 0.1 m/s, the temperature of LIB at the end of the cooling cycle is about 25.42 °C. However, when the velocity of water is increased to 0.3 m/s, the temperature of LIB cells is reduced to 25.24 °C, projecting a 0.74 % reduction. Similar reduction of 0.52 % is found for 1 % of Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid when fluid velocity changed from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s.

The cooling simulation data for the standard current discharge rate of 1C shows a similar trend when the fluid velocity is increased to 0.3 m/s. Fig. 16 illustrates the cooling curve of the LIB module at 0.3 m/s and 1C discharge rate. The maximum temperature of 25.75 °C was obtained for the LIB cell when cooled using water at a velocity of 0.3 m/s. This represents about a 1.47 % reduction in LIB temperature when the velocity of water is increased from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s. The lowest battery temperature of 25.32 °C was obtained using a 1 % volume concentration of hybrid nanofluid. Compared with the battery temperature results obtained for the fluid velocity of 0.1 m/s, a higher velocity of 0.3 m/s for 1 % Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid resulted in about 1.74 % improvement in cooling performance. A comparable improvement in cooling performance is observed in 1 % of Al2O3 and GO mono nanofluid which is about 1.74 %. Therefore, hybrid nanofluid (1 % of volume concentration) displays a temperature curve with a much lower gradient compared to the cooling curve of water. Furthermore, the temperature gradient of LIB, when cooled by a hybrid nanofluid at 1 % particle concentrations, displays almost a linear curve, indicating a temperature uniformity between multiple cells.

The effect of fluid velocity on LIB temperature reduction is more noticeable when using a hybrid nanofluid as a working fluid at a higher current discharge rate of 2C as depicted in Fig. 17 . This is revealed by the LIB temperature, which demonstrates a 7.53 % improvement for Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid (1 %) as the fluid velocity rises from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s. Even for mono nanofluid 1.0 % of Al2O3 and GO showed a similar percentage of enhancement which is around 7 % when fluid velocity increased from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s. The rise in fluid velocity increased the mobility of nanoparticles in base fluid and resulted in the rise of enabling a higher heat transfer rate. A similar effect of fluid velocity on LIB temperature rise was reported by Mahdi et al. [6] where they found that the maximum temperature is reduced by 1.02K when the inflow velocity was increased from 0.2 m/s to 0.28 m/s. Even though there is not much deviation in the LIB temperature at the end of the cooling cycle 900s for both mono nanofluid and hybrid nanofluids, the rise in battery temperature before the normalization period was extensively reduced.

The impact of cooling Re on LIB temperature at a discharge rates of 0.5C–2C for Re ranging between 450 and 2000 are shown in Figs. 18 and 19
. From Fig. 18, it can be observed that the highest battery temperature is about 31.25 °C for Re of 641 when water is employed at 2C discharge rate. Whereas, at the lowest Re of 478, the maximum battery temperature is approximately 25.22 °C for 1 % of Al2O3-GO. The increase in hybrid nanofluid concentration showed a reduction Re leading to improved cooling effect. Based on Fig. 19, at the highest and lowest Re of 1924 and 1435, the maximum battery temperature was 25.24 °C and 25.09 °C. Therefore, it can be observed that when the Re increased by almost three times, the battery temperature reduced approximately by 0.7 %. One thing to be noted from Figs. 18 and 19 is when the hybrid nanofluid volume concentration is increased, Re decreases due to the augmentation in fluid viscosity. Besides, the Re is significantly higher when cooling LIB at a higher current discharge rate. Nevertheless, the effect of Re on LIB temperature displayed almost a similar trend for both fluid velocities. Also, previous literature by Saghir et al. [45] mentioned that Re represents the fluid flow rate as performance evaluation criteria, particularly for low Re. Overall, the numerical investigation of the present study at fluid velocities of 0.1 and 0.3 m/s strongly indicates a higher temperature reduction for LIB cells at a high current discharge rate of 2C employing Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of current discharge rate on LIB temperature
ce:section.ce:para:
The increase in the current discharge rate increases the heat generation rate of LIB, accelerating the temperature rise. Without cooling, the LIB rises to a maximum of 29 °C, 32 °C and 36 °C for 0.5C, 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively. Figs. 20 and 21
illustrate the maximum temperature attained by LIB cells with a novel thermal management system tested using Al2O3-GO nanofluids and its base fluid at a fluid velocity of 0.1 m/s and 0.3 m/s, respectively. The trend line plotted in both graphs is based on the discharge rate of 2C. The increase in the current discharge rate resulted in higher temperature values for LIB cells for all working fluids investigated. For lower fluid velocity of 0.1 m/s, the maximum temperature difference of LIB between discharge rate of 0.5C and 2C when cooled with Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid is about 3.34 °C. Correspondingly, for mono Al2O3 and GO nanofluids, the maximum temperature difference of LIB is about 3.67 °C and 3.60 °C. However, when a similar comparison is made for water, the temperature difference of LIB between 0.5C and 2C discharge rate is clearly higher, about 5.83 °C. This proves that water is not suitable to be used for cooling LIB at a higher discharge rate. Therefore, the present data suggest that Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid with 1 % particle concentrations has superior performance at a higher battery discharge rate even with a low fluid velocity of 0.1 m/s.

The higher heat generation of LIB at a higher discharge rate was suppressed by the higher heat removal due to increased fluid velocity. The comparison between maximum battery temperature for a discharge rate of 0.5C and 1C displays a minimal variation due to higher fluid velocity. As shown in Fig. 21, the peak battery temperature for cooling with Al2O3-GO hybrid (1 % of volume concentration) for discharge rates of 0.5C, 1C and 2C is about 25.09 °C, 25.32 °C, and 26.56 °C. Meanwhile, the maximum temperature difference for battery cooling data using hybrid nanofluid for a discharge rate of 0.5C and 2C is about 5.53 %. Yet, for all discharge rates, the use of hybrid nanofluid can maintain the LIB cell temperature to be less than 29 °C, compared to the peak temperature of 36 °C without any cooling system. Therefore, the simulation data indicates that the increase in the current discharge rate does not affect the performance of Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid as a cooling medium for the LIB thermal management system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal behaviour of LIB with water and Al2O3-GO nanofluid cooling
ce:section.ce:para: Table 5 exhibits the LIB module's temperature contour, which represents the heat distribution over the battery surface when cooled with Al2O3-GO nanofluids and its base fluid at different fluid velocities and current discharge rates. Based on Table 5, the temperature contour for water is slightly different and mono, and hybrid nanofluids appear to have almost similar heat distribution. Half of the battery module temperatures are in the red zone when the battery is cooled with water. However, when cooled with mono and hybrid nanofluid, only the four batteries near the outlet of the channels are in the red zone, indicating a high simulation temperature with a fluid velocity of 0.1 m/s. Mainly this is due to the heat dissipation from the LIB to the cooling block towards the outlet area. Mostly, the batteries in the module are in the cool color region, which indicates the temperature is low and has an effective cooling effect. Furthermore, the temperature contour of the battery area near the inlet is mostly at a lower temperature gradient compared to the outlet area due to the heat dissipation by the coolant towards the outlet region. However, as the fluid velocity was increased from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s, the number of LIBs in the high temperature (red zone) region reduced as the rate of heat transferred by the cooling fluid increased significantly. Also, it can be observed that the battery temperature contour matches each discharge rate of 0.5C, 1C, and 2C. However, the battery temperature contour with a lower red region proves that the battery cooling performance is enhanced at a velocity of 0.3 m/s with Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid as a working fluid.

The thermal management system of the battery's function is to distribute even temperature around the battery and prevent the battery from overheating by releasing heat from the battery. The constant distribution of temperature is crucial for good control and better performance of the battery thermal management system. The temperature uniformity is basically the variance between the battery module and the average temperature of battery module which is measured quantitatively. Eventually, the temperature uniformity becomes zero when there is uniform heat disstribution among all cells. Fig. 22 demonstrates the battery temperature uniformity during different fluid flowing and it can be found that temperature uniformity is better for hybrid nanofluids compared to base fluid and mono nanofluid. At 900s the temperature uniformity of hybrid nanofluids in the cooling channel is 0.42 °C. When different cooling fluids are utilized, the maximum temperature uniformity difference is about 0.02 °C which does not make a huge difference. The present findings is significantly better than the results obtained by Kumar and Sarkar [40] when cylindrical microtube cooling system was used with temperature difference between cells of 0.367 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nanofluid on heat transfer coefficient and Nusselt number
ce:section.ce:para: The heat transfer coefficient obtained for water was compared to the results obtained with mono and hybrid. Fig. 23 shows the heat transfer coefficient increasing significantly for water, mono and hybrid nanofluid when fluid velocity increased from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s. It can be found that the heat transfer rate is raised by the fluid velocity increase and the discharge rate increase. For 0.5C, 1C, and 2C, the maximum heat transfer rate is improved by 17 %, 20 % and 23 %, respectively, when the velocity of 1.0 % of Al2O3-GO is increased. A similar enhancement percentage was found for other working fluids when velocity changed. It can be seen from Fig. 23 that the higher the fluid velocity, the higher the heat transfer coefficient. When comparing the working fluid at 0.1 m/s and 0.3 m/s, the highest concentration of hybrid nanofluid has the highest heat transfer coefficient among working fluids, which is 67 %, 45 % and 28 % higher than water, GO and Al2O3 mono nanofluid at 1C and 2C. It indicates a good thermal performance when compared to base fluid. A comparable result found in the literature by Vicki et al. [41] reported that the maximum heat transfer coefficient improved by 84.1 % when 1.0 % (50:50 ratio) of Al2O3-CuO nanofluid flow at 5 LPM flowrate and concluded 1.0 % volume fraction of Al2O3-CuO nanofluid has better heat transfer performance than base fluid.

Moreover, the greater the volume concentration of hybrid nanofluid, the higher the fluid heat transfer coefficient and this is due to the higher thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid. Plus, the thermal performance of the hybrid nanofluid is better when compared to base fluid and this is because of the Brownian motion influence on nanofluid thermophysical properties. The temperature influenced the nanoparticle's physical properties and increased the random motion inside the cooling channel which improves the heat transfer rate.

The calculated Nu from and heat transfer coefficient was compared within different working fluids at two different fluid velocities. Fig. 24 revealed the comparison of the Nu of all working fluid at 0.1 m/s and 0.3 m/s at 0.5C 1C and 2C discharge rates. The Nu also increases significantly when fluid velocity is raised. At 2C, the lowest Nu is for the base fluid which is about 6.52 and 7.67 while the highest Nu are 8.71 and 10.69 at 0.1 m/s and 0.3 m/s for 1.0 % of Al2O3-GO at 2C. The maximum Nu increased at 0.5C, 1C and 2C are around 1.37, 1.65 and 1.98 respectively when velocity increased from 0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s. It can be observed that the linear trend of water and 1.0 % of Al2O3-GO from Fig. 23 show a significant increase and this indicates that the Nu of every working fluid is improving with rise in discharge rate. At 0.3m/sthe Nu for 1.0 % of Al2O3-GO enhanced 1.17, 1.10 and 1.07 times more than base fluid, GO and Al2O3 mono nanofluid at higher C-rate. Vicki et al. [41]reported comparable Nu for 1 % volume fraction of Al2O3-CuO hybrid nanofluid about 66.7 % which is 1.66 times better than base fluid.

This paper explores the simulation work of a novel battery thermal management system when discharged at 0.5C, 1C and 2C discharge rates by using Al2O3-GO/water-based nanofluids. The numerical model of the LIB thermal management system was successfully validated with the experimental data. Overall, the following conclusions were drawn based on the experimental and numerical investigation.
•
The Al2O3-GO (1 % of volume concentration) hybrid nanofluid has the highest thermal conductivity value and moderate dynamic viscosity compared to mono nanofluids.
•
The increase in current discharge rate resulted in higher battery temperature.
•
The fluid velocity of 0.3 m/s provides a better cooling effect for LIB for all working fluids.
•
The Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid with 1 % particle concentrations produces the best cooling effect for LIB cells with a maximum temperature reduction of 10.6 °C.
•
A 42.5 % enhancement in thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluid able to reduce the temperature of LIB by almost 7.84 %.
•
The numerical model is validated with experimental data with a maximum deviation of less than 1 %.
•
The LIB module achieved superior temperature uniformity with a minimal temperature difference between cells of 0.42 °C highlighting even heat dissipation.
•
The increase in fluid velocity and nanoparticle suspension improves the heat transfer coefficient and Nu.

Concisely, the highest volume concentration of 1 % Al2O3-GO hybrid nanofluid is suitable for battery LIB cooling, and it is highly recommended for use in the thermal management system of 18650 cylindrical LIB cells.

Khesarubini Selvarajoo: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. V Vicki Wanatasanappan: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Ng Yee Luon: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Abhinav Kumar: Visualization, Project administration, Methodology. Prabhu Paramasivam: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Ashish Agrawal: Software, Resources. Abinet Gosaye Ayanie: Supervision, Software, Project administration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: The research conducted in this study adhered to the ethical guidelines of the Journal. All participants provided informed consent to participate in this research. Informed consent was obtained in writing from all participants, and they were informed about the purpose of the study, potential risks, and their rights to withdraw their participation at any time without consequence.
Not Applicable.
The data used to support the findings of this study are included in the article. Should further data or information be required, these are available from the corresponding author upon request.
The authors declared that no funding was received for Publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Also, the author appreciates the financial support provided by UNITEN through the postgraduate excellence scholarship.
